---
title: In-memory cache
description: The default `Cache` — a plain in-process `Map` with lazy per-entry TTL. No size cap, no background sweep, single-process only; lost on restart. Right for tests, dev, and single-node setups.
---

`InMemoryCache` is the **default** `Cache` implementation.  It's a
thin wrapper over a plain `Map` — in-process, zero dependencies,
lazy per-entry expiry.  There is no eviction policy and no
background sweeper: it does what a `Map` does, plus TTL.

```ts
import { InMemoryCache } from 'actor-ts';

const cache = new InMemoryCache();
```

The constructor takes **no options** — there is nothing to tune.
A TTL is supplied per call (see [TTL handling](#ttl-handling)),
not at construction time.

## When to use it

Three scenarios:

1. **Tests** — fast, no IO, clean teardown via `close()`.
2. **Single-process production** — one process, no need to share
   cache state across pods.
3. **Dev / local** — the same code without Redis on the laptop.

For **multi-process** deployments, use [Redis](/cache/redis/) or
[Memcached](/cache/memcached/) instead — each process would
otherwise keep its own independent copy.

## The operations

`InMemoryCache` implements the full [`Cache`](/cache/overview/)
surface:

```ts
await cache.set('user:1', user, 60_000);            // value + optional TTL (ms)
const hit = await cache.get<User>('user:1');        // Option<User> — None on miss/expiry

await cache.setIfAbsent('lock:job', '1', 30_000);   // true iff it was stored
await cache.incr('ratelimit:1.2.3.4', 60_000);      // atomic ++, returns the new count

const many = await cache.mget<User>(['user:1', 'user:2']);  // Map<string, User>
await cache.mset(new Map([['a', user1], ['b', user2]]), 60_000);

await cache.delete('user:1', 'user:2');             // one or many keys
await cache.close();                                // clears the Map
```

`get` returns an `Option<V>` — `None` on a miss or after expiry.

## TTL handling

```ts
await cache.set('key', value, 60_000);   // expires at now + 60s
await cache.set('key', value);           // no TTL — lives until deleted
```

Expiry is **lazy**: an entry's deadline is checked on every `get`,
`mget`, `incr`, and `setIfAbsent`, and an expired entry is dropped
at that point.  There is **no background sweeper** — an expired
entry that is never touched again stays in the `Map` until you
`delete` it or `close()` the cache.

`incr` sets the TTL only when it **creates** the counter (the value
becomes 1); later increments don't refresh it — the right
semantics for a fixed-window rate limiter.

## No eviction

`InMemoryCache` has **no size cap and no LRU** — it holds whatever
you put in it until the entry expires (lazily) or you remove it.
For a bounded working set that fits in memory, that's exactly what
you want.  For a workload that writes many distinct, never-re-read
keys, give every entry a TTL (or `delete` it) so the `Map` doesn't
grow without bound.

## Sharing across the system

```ts
import { CacheExtensionId, InMemoryCache } from 'actor-ts';

// The extension's `default` cache is an InMemoryCache out of the box:
const cache = system.extension(CacheExtensionId).cache();

// Override the default, or register a separate named cache:
system.extension(CacheExtensionId).setCache('default', new InMemoryCache());
const sessions = system.extension(CacheExtensionId).cache('sessions');
```

`system.extension(CacheExtensionId).cache(name)` resolves a cache
by name — the `default` name is an `InMemoryCache` unless you
replace it (via `setCache`, `registerCache`, or the HOCON path
`actor-ts.cache.<name>.plugin`).  HTTP middleware, projection
actors, and your own code then share one configured instance
instead of each building its own.

For a throwaway cache, just construct `new InMemoryCache()`
directly — no extension needed.

## When it's wrong for production

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

<Aside type="caution" title="Multi-process state divergence">
  ```ts
  // 4 pods, each with its own InMemoryCache → 4 independent caches
  ```
  The same request hitting different pods can get a cached response
  from one and miss on another.  Use Redis for shared multi-process
  state.
</Aside>

<Aside type="caution" title="Restart loses everything">
  ```bash
  # kubectl delete pod my-app-1
  # → the entire pod's cache is gone
  ```
  In-memory caches don't survive restart.  For caches where the
  cold-start cost is significant (rebuilding takes minutes), use
  Redis (persists) or accept the cold start.
</Aside>

<Aside type="caution" title="No cap — set TTLs to bound growth">
  ```ts
  // No maxEntries, no LRU, no sweeper: a Map that only grows.
  await cache.set(key, value);              // ✗ lives forever until deleted
  await cache.set(key, value, 300_000);     // ✓ reclaimed lazily after 5 min
  ```
  Give entries a TTL (or `delete` them) on write-heavy, low-reuse
  workloads — nothing evicts them for you.
</Aside>
